items.caption: Happy 4th, yall 🌭✨🇺🇸
items.transcript: So one day, around 250 years ago, there was a group of people who had the audacity to do something impossible. The country that you live in, that you were born into, the United States of America, it didn't always exist. It used to be run by a different country, an empire, the largest, most powerful empire in all the land. And that empire had a king. And they didn't really like how things were being run. So they thought, what if we created our own country? A country where people could be free, free to say whatever they want, free to practice whatever religion they want, where there was liberty and justice for all. It was a radical idea. They said, we believe in this so much we're going to go to war for it. And they did. Normal people like me and you, they went and they fought for this idea that there could be a country where people govern themselves. It was never going to work. But you know what? They did win. And they created the United States of America. And it's not a perfect country, but what makes it such a great country is that people don't settle for the status quo and we're always fighting to make it better. And that's what makes somebody an American. We aren't united by our government, we're united by our values. And it changed the world forever. And so that's why we celebrate today. Not because we're celebrating politicians or government, but we're celebrating the people who had the audacity to not settle for the status quo, to go do something amazing, but to go try to change the world for the better. Happy 4th of July, little man. Go have the audacity. You got this.

items.caption:
Hiiii 👋 just your freshly postpartum friendly neighborhood career-oriented girl who was apprehensive about pregnancy and scared of childbirth checking in to share her positive experience!!! 10/10 recommend - on cloud 9! ☁️ 

Am I randomly weeping? Yes. 
Does my body hurt? Yes. 
Am I anxious about the next few months? Yes. 
But it’s all figure-out-able — especially with the right partner + prep 🤍🤍🤍
items.transcript: Hey guys, I whispered because everybody's sleeping. I gave birth like 48 hours ago. I know that this is all going to be a blur so I just wanted to give some initial thoughts that I'm having. My labor experience was way more fun and relaxing than I ever imagined. I did not know that it could be that enjoyable. If you're somebody who needs a positive birth story, I am your girl. I was having false labor for a week and I was able to get my contractions to actually stick by pumping and I ate spicy Chinese food the night before and I swear that helped them to stick. We got admitted at like 11am. We got the epidural around 6pm and then I just like vibed out with my mom, my sister, my husband. Just had like a good time. I took a massive nap. They woke me up and were like, hey you want a pup? And I was like, sure. Pushed them out in 20 minutes. Completely 100% painless. I could feel everything but zero pain. It was amazing. Number two, some things that I'm happy we brought. Very cozy throw blankets. We brought our own pillows from home. Like cozy pajamas and lounge wear. Just like lots of like comfort items. The Freedom Mom everything. The postpartum kit for sure. It's a very un-chic experience. Just feel a little bit more dignified. One thing that I forgot is all my like nursing stuff. So if you're planning on nursing, like bring your stuff with you. Lastly, I just remembered these two phrases and they were really really helpful. First one is this is supposed to be hard. Every contraction, every single piece of pain is just getting you closer to your goal. It's supposed to be hard. It's one of the hardest things a person can go through. The second thing is this is all temporary. So even now as we are on our breastfeeding journey, I'm remembering that everything is temporary. A breakthrough is on the horizon. I can't wait to get home and shower and see my dog. I feel so energized and happy and really really really lucky. And there's my Starbucks. You want this?

“Follow your passion” = worst career advice ever 👎

But no need to fear, AdviceWithErin is here!!! 😎

We actually kind of sort of invented an app that helps you find your “dream job” ✨

It’s called StupidFish & it helps you discover hidden career opportunities & matches you with ideal career paths 📲

Find your dream life & then a job that fits INTO that life. You got this 👏 #careeradvice 

Type “FISH” for the link & see for yourself!! 😍🫧🐟
items.transcript: Ugh, I don't know what I want to do with my life. Well, what are you passionate about? Well, I really love singing. I mean, being a singer is like my dream job. Follow your passion. Do what you love and you'll never work a day in your life. Oh, yeah. So, um, that's terrible advice. Well, why? I mean, if you do what you love, it doesn't feel like work. Well, first, just because you're passionate about something doesn't really mean that you're good at it. No offense. Not taken. I'm a mediocre singer at best. And second, if you turn what you love into work, especially if you're not good at it, it may quickly become something that you hate. So what? Are we supposed to encourage kids to do what they're not passionate about? Hey, kids, go do something that you hate. No, not at all. You have to figure out what you're uniquely good at. Like, what's something that comes easy to you that other people find challenging? Well, what about dream jobs then? All right, here's the truth about dream jobs. A dream job is any job that allows you to live your dream life. A lot of people are quick to pick a job and then try to morph their life around that job. But my advice? Figure out what's important to you. What kind of life do you want to live? And then find a job that allows you to live that life. And that, my friend, is your dream job. Well, while I do really love to sing, I am actually really talented at anything having to do with quantum computing or AI model training. I don't know, it just comes really easy to me. Oh, well then, yes, please, follow your talent so that you can sing your heart out on your 70-foot yacht one day. You got this? And follow for more.

items.caption: Type “FISH” to try it 🐟
Passing alo advice that was shared with me 🫶✨ #postpartum #lifeadvice #firsttimemom
items.transcript: I'm due like any second now. And as much as I'm ready for this to be over, I've also had my friends warn me. They're like errands. So, um, postpartum? Yeah. Buckle up. And so this is me buckling up. And five things that I will be doing to proactively make my postpartum be as comfy cozy as possible. Okay, number one. I will be tattooing this phrase on the inside of my eyeballs. And that phrase is, it is temporary. It is all temporary. The ups and the downs. The goods and the bads. Because one day, lord willing, that screaming, crying baby will be graduating high school. And one day, lord willing, hopefully a lot sooner than that, I will be back on the beach drinking margaritas with my girlfriends in a bikini. It will happen. Because it is all temporary. Number two. Curbing screen time. I can see how a lot of people get addicted to their phones in postpartum. You're isolating your house. You're up all night by yourself. You're Googling stuff. You're worried. You're anxious. You're concerned. So, I have downloaded some books on the Kindle. And I have set some boundaries and some rules on myself. I will not be Googling random things at 3 a.m. I will be texting my friends who have had kids and calling my doctor. No doom scrolling allowed. Number three. I bought some cute cozy sets. Some pajamas. Some loungewear. Because I believe when you look good, you feel good. All of my pants with zippers, they're on a vacation. Let's stick to the cute and comfy. Number four. I have my paid and unpaid village set up. I already know that I'm going to be a wreck over my dog. So, I've asked my mom to come over and walk my dog. Unpaid. Thanks, mom. And then we also have our paid village. We have a housekeeper who comes every other week. And I know that's not realistic for everybody. But I think it's important for me to tell you that. Because if you see my videos and you're like, wow, Erin, your house looks so clean postpartum. That's not me. That's Michael and a professional. Okay? Working together. And number five. I am not comparing. We're not comparing. Not comparing recovery. Not comparing babies. You know, a lot of it's just a roll of the dice. A luck of the draw. Genetics. We need to be comparing. We are all on our own journey. Oh, and bonus tip y'all gave me. I bought an ungodly amount of snacks from Costco. Because y'all were like, oh, you think you're hungry now? Just wait until you're nursing. And that scared me. Because I'm really hungry right now. You got this. And follow for more.

items.caption:
🍻 reposting my dads viral video from a couple years ago when we were 4 days deep to camping in Yosemite lol

Happy Father’s Day to the man who gives AdviceWithErin advice (and my sense of humor lol) — my dad!

And to all the dads out there who are working hard to be the best person they can be for their kids - you’re doing great! You got this 👏

#fathersday #advice #planningbeer
items.transcript: Can you explain to the listeners what a planning beer is in concept and why it's so important? I lived with Jack, Jack, and Joe, I mean, when I was in college, and nights we were going to go out for a beer, we'd have to have a conversation about where to go for a beer. We never seemed to be able to come to any agreement, and we'd stand next to the door, which was in our kitchen, above a drugstore, and we'd have these long, frustrating conversations about where to go for a beer, and then one day, I forget which one of us said, wait a minute, why don't we have a beer while we plan where we're going to go have a beer? Why don't we have a planning beer? And it was just one of those, like I'm 66, and it might have been one of the most important. Confucius says wisdom is the correct naming of things. And that day, my life changed, because from then on, we were never frustrated trying to figure out where to go to have a beer. We had a planning beer while we talked about having a beer, but as the beer went down in the bottle, the time we gave ourselves to come to a conclusion, it was almost like an hourglass, because when we got to the end of our planning beer, we really had to decide where we were going to go have a beer. Does that make sense? Yeah, and then what do you do when you're done with your planning beer and you still have not decided? Well, that's a good point. I mean, lesser people might just say, okay, that's what we're going to do, let's run out the door. But now that the planning beer was empty, we created the transition beer.

items.caption: let’s toss rollercoasters in there while we’re at it 🎢 #thirdtrimester #pregnant
items.transcript: I'm 38 weeks pregnant and here are some things that I cannot wait to do. Cannot wait to do when I'm not pregnant anymore. Number one, a really good core workout. Not right away, I know, but eventually I'm gonna get back in solid core and it's gonna be the worst workout class of my life and I'm gonna love it. Number two, tretinoin. Need I say more? Number three, the crispest buttery chardonnay with two ice cubes. A smoky mezcal margarita. The juiciest double IPA. And a tart orange crush if you're from Maryland, you know. What else? Oh, tuna tartare. I'm coming for you. I'm gonna eat like a pound. A pound of tartare. To sleep on my stomach? Oh, I'm going to just sleep on my stomach. Everywhere. Oh, hot tubs. Just like hot things in general, like saunas and stuff. I mean, it's like a sauna outside right now, but it's like not fun when you're pregnant. Oh, also just like adventurous things. Like I want to go on a big bike ride. Like a big 10 mile long bike ride. I know, not right away, okay? Not right away, ow. But like eventually, like when you're pregnant, you're just like, I'm so fragile. Like I'm so excited to not be fragile anymore.

items.caption: Where my third trimester ladies at? 🤗 #thirdtrimester #38weeks
items.transcript: Come with me to my 38-week appointment while we talk about the weird-ass symptoms that have been happening to me at the end of pregnancy that I wasn't prepared for but my friends probably told me about but I just wasn't listening. Number one, whatever happy hormone is getting secreted in my brain right now, it is like natural Xanax. I have so many reasons to be grumpy. I'm not sleeping. I'm not eating well. Everything hurts. I run my own businesses, which is stressful, but for some reason, I seem to be coping pretty well. So there's something going on in there. Number two, I have this perpetual cough. Every now and then, I'll just have a coughing fit where I legitimately sound like a chain smoker. It's just weird. Number three, I've had zero stomach issues. I have not had the lava throat, acid reflux, none of that. And what's crazy is that I have chronic stomach issues. Typical issues have gone away. I don't know if it's because the prostaglandin or the relaxin or whatever your body secretes when you're pregnant, but for some reason, I've been good. I feel like it's going to come back with a vengeance. Number four, facial swelling and like neck swelling. Everything's swelling. My hand's swelling. Everything's blowing up. It's 90 degrees outside. That doesn't help. My teeth have turned like blue. So I don't know what that's about. Number five, rib pain. Okay, I don't know if it's just because I'm a short girlie. I got a short torso, but oh my God. Number six, pelvic pain. Look, the girlie's warned me. They're like, you're gonna have pelvic pain the last three weeks. And I was like, I don't know what that means. Now I know what that means. Your pelvis starts to fall apart. And I go to a pelvic floor PT and I'm like, what do I do? What do I do? Should I do yoga? And she's like, no, you just kind of have to let your body fall apart. Everything has to get out of the way. And then we can put you back together. Number seven, ending on a high note. I am ravenous. I have not had an appetite this whole pregnancy. The last half of the third trimester, oh my goodness. Food tastes so good and I want it all the time. Unfortunately, I have no space, no space to eat. But I'm enjoying everything I'm eating. I will have a milkshake and I will drink the whole thing and I won't give a damn. All right, maybe I'll see you guys next week. Bye.

items.transcript: There

items.caption:
I got so many requests asking for a breakdown of the new “trump accounts” - so here you go! 🍼💸🤓

You got this! 👏 

#personalfinance #parent #money #trumpaccounts
items.transcript: Hey Erin, have you heard about these Trump account things where you can get free money for your baby? Should I open one? So, Section 530A accounts, also known as Trump accounts, become available on July 4th, 2026. Here's what you need to know. Number one, if your baby is a U.S. citizen born between these dates, they are eligible for a one-time $1,000 federal seat deposit funded by the U.S. Treasury, aka your tax dollars. You can claim it by filing an election on your kid's behalf by going to this website. Number two, you or your family can contribute up to $5,000 a year combined. It's not tax deductible, and it must be invested in a low-cost U.S. stock index fund. Number three, there are no withdrawals at all before the age of 18. After 18, it's basically treated like a traditional IRA, so if your kid wants to take the money out, the seed and the growth is all taxed at regular income. Wait, wait, wait, wait, I heard that the kid can take the money out for free if they want to start their own business or buy a house. Yeah, so things like that have been said, but if you read closely, a first-home withdrawal skips the penalty but is still taxed, and then there's no start-up business exception at all, so that one is taxed and penalized. Well, do you think that I should do it or not? So I can't give you investing advice, but I can give you information so that you can make an informed decision. There are already a ton of great ways to invest for your kid in the U.S. Custodial Roth IRAs and 529 accounts generally have much better tax advantages and flexibility, so a lot of people don't really understand why the 530A was invented in the first place, but it's there until 2028 if you want to go take advantage of it. Also, the IRS says that your employer can actually contribute up to $2,500 a year, and for them, it's tax deductible, but not for you. Do your research, look at all your options, and then make the decision that's best for your family. Thanks, Erin. I feel so informed now.

items.caption: @stupidfish.app helps you figure it out. Comment “FISH” to give it a try 🐟
items.transcript: Oh yes, oh yes, oh yes, they both.

items.caption: How I launched an app in the App Store with ZERO technical experience 🙂‍↕️🙂‍↕️🙂‍↕️
items.transcript: Last week, my team released an app that got tens of thousands of users within our first week. So in the next two minutes, I'm going to tell you exactly how I created an app with no coding experience, step by step. Step number one, I didn't want to create an app. I wanted to solve a problem. You should never create a business or try to start an app for the sake of doing it. You should always be looking to solve a problem. The problem that I wanted to solve? The unequal access to great career opportunities. Step number two, wireframing. This is basically just when you draw out how your app will work. Then I brought it into Figma Make to vibe code a prototype. And then I asked my friends in product and UX UI design for their feedback. Number three, finding a technical partner. I'm the career advice girl. I don't know how to write a line of code. And Stupid Fish was a very ambitious idea. So I spent a few months looking for a technical co-founder. I found one. Shout out to Y Combinator. And we worked together to see if Stupid Fish was even feasible. Spoiler alert, it works. Step four, design. Y'all, if you were trying to create an app, please hire a human being to design it. I can sniff a vibe-coded app or website from a mile away. They all look the same. And people can feel it. My husband, Michael, actually came on as the head of design. And while he has a decade of brand and design experience, he had never designed an app. And he basically taught himself front-end development with Cloud Code within a matter of weeks. Step number five, testing. We did a lot of beta fish testing, as we called it. Get it? Some of you watching may have even been in our batches. Stupid Fish is very technically complex, so we needed to do a lot of testing to figure out what was working, what wasn't. And it was painful, but it is necessary. And step number six, we hired human beings. There's this idea of the billion-dollar, 1% AI company. And I just think that that's so boring. Sure, we could have had AI totally generate our illustrations, and probably nobody would have noticed or said anything. But instead, we decided to commission a human artist to come in and make our little illustrations. And look at how cute they are. When you're designing an app, a great way to make it look like what everybody else is doing is to do what everybody else is doing. So don't. Focus on solving a problem. Efficient is not always better. Good things take thought and time. So if you have yet to try out Stupid Fish, it's now available on Apple and Android. You got this.

items.caption:
yes I’m filming from my couch I’m 9 months pregnant ya girl is doing her BEST 🙂‍↕️ 

Find a partner who admires you & you admire back. That’s it. That’s the formula. 

You got this 👏
items.transcript: To all the girls and younger men out there who have been spending time on social media and have seen that trend where it's like, I'm at Pilates at 10 a.m. because I didn't marry a loser or I'm home with my kids because I didn't marry a loser. I need you to watch this video. Please, please listen closely. As somebody who is in a very, very happy relationship, very strong marriage, I need you to know that how much money your man makes has absolutely nothing to do with him being a loser or not. It also has nothing to do with him being a provider or not. There are men out there who wake up every day, go work hard at their jobs, come home, and they simply just don't make enough to fund their household on one income because things are expensive right now. And then there are guys, definitely guys, out there who make hundreds of thousands of dollars a year. They're worth millions of dollars and they are 100% complete losers. Like losers. That's because what makes a man a loser isn't how much money he does or does not make. Because what an actual loser husband looks like is a guy who doesn't respect you. A guy who doesn't admire you. He doesn't celebrate you. He doesn't see you as an equal. He doesn't listen to you. And that has nothing to do with how much money a guy makes. There are so many women at their 10 a.m. Pilates classes who are in miserable marriages. So I'm team don't marry a loser. But you gotta know what a loser actually looks like.

items.caption: idc about KPIs i’m 3D printing a human who’s going to violently claw their way out in a matter of days (or weeks)
items.transcript: I have a busy week. Thursday, Nobu, Vegas Friday. Then I have a spray tan at 8:45.

probably should’ve gatekept this, oh well 😌

So we needed to do a lot of testing to figure out what was working, what wasn't. And it was painful, but it is necessary. And step number six, we hired human beings. There's this idea of the billion dollar one person AI company. And I just think that's so boring. Sure, we could have had AI totally generate our illustrations and probably nobody would have noticed or said anything. But instead, we decided to commission a human artist to come in and make our little illustrations. And look at how cute they are. When you're designing an app, a great way to make it look like what everybody else is doing is to do what everybody else is doing. So don't. Focus on solving a problem. Efficient is not always better. Good things take thought and time. So if you have yet to try out Stupid Fish, it's now available on Apple and Android. You got this.

items.caption: Your success is inevitable 👏
items.transcript: So last night, I watched a docuseries on my guy Abraham Lincoln, as one does. If you are somebody who feels behind in life, like you missed your shot, like your life is just locked in and this is all it's ever gonna be, let me give you some career advice from my good old friend Abe. So something that a lot of people don't know about Abraham Lincoln is that he was kind of a failure. Like he would not have been on Forbes 30 under 30. He was born into utter poverty. Like he was feral. He had less than one year formal education his entire life. And up until he's 22, he just kind of worked on his dad's farm. And then he weasels his way into co-owning this general store where he literally sells whiskey. And he's pretty like socially popular, so he decides to run for state legislature in Illinois. And he loses. Oh, and then the general store goes under. So he's 23, no college degree, a failure in his career so far, and straddled with debt. So he's 31 now, he engaged to Mary Todd, but then your boy crashes out. He spirals into a deep clinical depression, and he describes himself as, quote, the most miserable man living. It was bad. 33, he's weathered the storm, he marries Mary Todd, and he's working as a lawyer in Illinois. At this point, he is forgettable in history books. No one will remember Abraham Lincoln. At age 37, he finally wins a seat in the U.S. House. At age 46, we are now in his mid-40s, he decides to run for U.S. Senate. And guess what? He loses. But it's okay, because at age 49, he decides to run again. And guess what happens this time? He loses. Again. We are now in his 50s. At age 51, Abe finally gets his big break, and he gets elected to president. Major blowup. And we all know what happens from there, okay? Emancipation Proclamation, Gettysburg Address, Civil War, all that stuff. Now imagine if he gave up. Imagine if at age 23 or 35 or 47, he had thrown in the towel and said, maybe nobody cares what old Abe has to say. Maybe this is it for me. The world would be a different place. Listen, come here. You can be successful. You can make an impact. The world doesn't care about how much time you feel like you've wasted or how behind you feel. The world is in desperate need of good people doing good things. So please, I am begging you, be the hero of your own story. Don't wait. Be your own role model. Set the example for everyone else. It is never too late to change the course of your life. Your good future is out there waiting for you. And I can't wait to see what you do. You got this. And follow for more.

items.transcript: This is your sign to be a little toxic in your next job interview. Let me explain. From 2022 to 2025, there was a frenzy of AI investment. And now in the great year of our Lord 2026, it's time for that to start paying off. Companies need to deliver. And you can use this to your advantage. All those layoffs they did to replace people with AI, all those AI demos they showed to clients promising new features and products, AI is so powerful, they said. And we have the solutions. Well, time's up. Time to start delivering. AI's gotta start making that money. So every company right now has top down initiatives. So if you go into any job interview right now and you bring up these initiatives, gold, money, five stars. But Erin, how do I even figure out what these initiatives are? One word, LinkedIn. LinkedIn is your secret. Every CEO of every company posts on LinkedIn, like a lot. It's like they're being held at gunpoint. So go to LinkedIn, type in the company name, read their posts. What's top of mind for the leadership? What's the strategy? What's the focus? What's the anxiety? And use that against them. So poke around, see what's going on, leave a couple comments. Really love this angle, Bob. Shelly, great points all around. You lurk. Oh, this B2B SaaS company is moving their focus from enterprise to SMBs? Interesting. You go into the interview, see I noticed the CEO recently posted about this on LinkedIn. Is this a major initiative right now? And they say, yes, actually it is. You say, well, with my previous experience in X, I'd be more than equipped to assist with that. Boom, done, hired. All companies have anxiety right now. So if you know how to use that against them, like a toxic little troll, in a truthful and honest way, of course. You got this. And follow for more.

items.caption:
This video is over 3 minutes so apparently IG won’t show it to anyone lol but there’s no quicker way to truncate a nuanced 32-page study & the misinformation was driving me nuts 😅 — I read the study, here’s what you need to know! And I def encourage you to read the study for yourself 🫶🏼

You got this 👏 #stanford #hiring #ai
items.transcript: The Stanford Institute for Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence just released a study that is going completely viral because it's exposing the secret AI hiring algorithm that gives people a resume score that follows them around from company to company and is causing people to get mass auto-rejected before the companies even see their application. Just kidding. The study literally doesn't say that at all. And anybody who's making videos saying that clearly just hasn't read the study and is just trying to go viral. The study barely even mentions resumes. I know that because I actually read it. So let me tell you what it actually says and what it means for your job search. If we haven't met before, hi, I'm Erin, also known as a vice with Erin and the founder of Stupid Fish. So this was a huge study. They followed 4 million people as they submitted 3.4 million job applications to 1,700 jobs across 11 sectors. But the data was empirical, meaning they weren't running experiments. The entire study is based on data from one company, which we're going to talk about later. So when you apply for a job online, most of the time your application is first going to be screened by a third-party software. This is because applying to jobs has become much, much easier. With things like quick apply, mass apply, globalization, remote work, companies are getting inundated with applications. And so they use this third-party software to help them organize applications. This software has been around for decades and honestly, it's always been problematic. Now, the interesting thing is that there are a handful of companies that dominate the space. Companies like Workday, Greenhouse, Bamboo. And just like every other company on earth, they're building in AI tools. However, every company that uses this software sets it up differently. So that doesn't mean just because they're using the software, they're using AI. You follow me? It also doesn't mean that this software is actually AI. Everybody thinks everything is AI now. People will be like, oh, my dishwasher has AI. And I'm like, that's not AI, Brad. That's Bluetooth. It's been around for decades. I digress. Also, this study didn't even look at any of those companies. They didn't have access to their data. The entire study is based around one company called Pymetrics. It's a talent platform that some companies might use to assess candidates by having them participate in gameplay. Basically using little games to assess their personality type and stuff. So what the study actually found is that candidates who apply to jobs via Pymetrics complete the assessment. They have those results impact all their jobs they apply to using Pymetrics via this recommend versus do not recommend feature that they offer. That's it. Is it problematic if it doesn't work? Yeah. Could other software companies be practicing something similar? We don't know. We don't have insight into that. And that is why these researchers' takeaway and recommendation is not stop using AI. It's we need more transparency into what is going on. Now, something the study did find that I don't hear anybody talking about, yet it's the major takeaway from the study, is a pattern of racial discrimination. The study found that 26% of black applicants and 15% of Asian applicants apply to at least one position where the algorithm recommended them at a lower rate than the most favorite group, which is usually white applicants. The crazy thing is when you average all the data out, you don't see the racial bias. However, what these researchers did is they looked at it role by role and they found something really interesting. A black person may be more likely to be recommended for a warehouse job that already employs a large amount of black people, while a white person may be higher recommended to work at a bank if they already employed more white people. This is problematic. This is something we need to be talking about. Hiring is so inefficient and we have to figure out solutions. So my practical advice in the meantime, one, apply to smaller companies. They can't afford to use these fancy software, so they just review things manually. Two, network. The easiest way to get around all this computer slop mess is just to talk directly to a human being. You got this and follow for more.
